coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymers: A facile bioprobe for fluorescent determination of caffeic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaoman
Xu, Guanhong
Wei, Fangdi
Cen, Yao
Shi, Menglan
Cheng, Xia
Chai, Yuying
Sohail, Muhammad
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: A new and sensitive method for fluorescent determination of caffeic acid (CA) was proposed based on silane-functionazed carbon dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@MIPs). CDs@MIPs were prepared by using CA as the templates on the surface of silane-functionazed carbon dots(CDs) with a sol–gel process. The as-prepared CDs@MIPs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs@MIPs exhibited distinguished selectivity and high binding affinity to CA templates, and also showed good reusability. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs decreased linearly with the increase of CA in the range of 0.5–200 μM. The limit of detection was 0.11 μM (3σ/K). Finally, the proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of CA in human plasma. The result indicates that the method built has promising potential for monitoring CA concentration in clinic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Caffeic acid
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of saline-functionalized CDs and CDs@MIPs
Detection of CA in human plasma

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61775099 , 21705080 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20171487 , BK20171043 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of silane-functionalized CDs; (B) FT-IR spectra of APTES and silane-functionalized CDs; (C) XPS spectrum of silane-functionalized CDs; (D) absorption and fluorescence spectra of silane-functionalized CDs (excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 450 nm).
(A) TEM image of CDs@MIPs; absorption, (B) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs@MIPs; (C) fluorescence stability property of CDs@MIPs.

(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs@MIPs before (a) and after (b) templates extraction, and CDs@NIPs(c); (B) fluorescence spectra of CDs@NIPs (a), CDs@MIPs before (b) and after (c) templates extraction, (d) CDs@MIPs with addition of 50 μM CA.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs@MIPs (A) and CDs@NIPs (B) with addition of the indicated concentrations of CA; (C) the linear relationship between the quenching efficiency of CDs@MIPs or CDs@NIPs and the concentration of CA. (D) Reversible fluorescence quenching and recovery cycles of CDs@MIPs upon the repeated addition (the up dots) and removal (the bottom dots) of CA.
Selectivity of CDs@MIPs for CA and other interferents.
Illustration of the preparation of CDs@MIPs.
Comparison between the proposed method and other reported CA measures.
Determination of CA in human plasma with CDs@MIPs biosensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymers: A facile bioprobe for fluorescent determination of caffeic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new and sensitive method for fluorescent determination of caffeic acid (CA) was proposed based on silane-functionazed carbon dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@MIPs). CDs@MIPs were prepared by using CA as the templates on the surface of silane-functionazed carbon dots(CDs) with a sol–gel process. The as-prepared CDs@MIPs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs@MIPs exhibited distinguished selectivity and high binding affinity to CA templates, and also showed good reusability. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs decreased linearly with the increase of CA in the range of 0.5–200 μM. The limit of detection was 0.11 μM (3σ/K). Finally, the proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of CA in human plasma. The result indicates that the method built has promising potential for monitoring CA concentration in clinic.

ce:section.ce:para: Caffeic acid (3,4-dihydroxycinnamic acid, CA) is a kind of phenolic acid with unique antioxidant properties due to its two hydroxyl groups. It is reported that CA has various functions like anti-inflammatory, anti-tumor and immunomodulatory [1–3]. Diseases such as upper respiratory tract infections in children and obesity problems can be prevented by CA according to latest researches [4,5]. Due to these excellent pharmacological functions, CA has been used for clinical treatment and healthcare in the last few years. In modern medicine, CA is widely used in the therapy of leucopenia and thrombocytopenia as well as hemostasis [6]. However, some reports show that excess CA has carcinogenic effects [7]. Different analytical methods including GC–MS [8], high-performance liquid chromatography(HPLC) [9,10], electrochemical techniques [11] and bienzymatic sensors [12] have been developed for the determination of CA. But these methods need complex sample pretreatment, lengthy analysis time, and high costs. Thus, it is urgent and necessary to develop simple, sensitive and efficient platforms to monitor the concentration of CA in plasma.

Carbon dots (CDs), a superior class of nanomaterials derived from carbon materials, have attracted much attention since their discovery. CDs have been widely exploited to achieve high sensitive detection in biosensing because of their excellent photoluminescence property, high biocompatibility, simple synthetic routes as well as tunable surface functionalities [13]. The main obstacles in the development of CDs-based sensors are associated with their selectivity to targets.

In the past few years, molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs) have been widely used to combine with CDs to improve the selectivity of CDs-based sensors, which have displayed obvious advantages when detecting trace substances in complicated matrix [14,15]. MIPs can selectively recognize templates by assembling templates with functional monomers and cross-linking monomers [16]. After the templates are removed by eluting, the cavities with high specificity and affinity to the targets are exposed [17,18]. Recent years, MIPs have been widely used in sensor field because of its simplicity, reliability, and low cost method of fabrication [19,20]. To the best of our knowledge, a platform based on MIPs with photoluminescent CDs as matrix for CA determination has not yet been reported.

In this study, we combined the high selectivity of MIPs and the strong and stable photoluminescence of CDs to develop a facile method for the determination of CA. First, silane-functionalized CDs, which offered the signal of fluorescence and act as the carrier for next imprinting procedure, were synthesized by using citric acid as carbon source and aminosilane as coordinating solvent. After that, silane-functionazed CDs coated with molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@MIPs) were synthesized via a one-pot sol–gel process using CA as templates. The prepared CDs@MIPs bonded selectively with CA molecular, and the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs was quenched due to the electron transfer between CA and CDs@MIPs. Therefore, a platform to detect CA was successfully developed with outstanding performances such as label-free, easy to prepare and high selectivity compared with traditional detective methods.

Caffeic acid (CA, 98%), 3-aminopropyl-triethoxy-silane (APTES), and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Citric acid anhydrous, ammonia hydroxide (25% in water), glucose, galactose, glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), and bull serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). CaCl2, NaCl, MgCl2, Methanol, ethanol and acetic acid (HAc) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water was obtained from a PURELAB Classic water purification system (PALL, USA).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan). FT-IR spectra were measured by a Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a resolution of 2 cm−1 and a spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1. Absorption spectra were obtained by a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All the optical measurements were carried out under ambient temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by an Escalab250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA).

The saline-functionalized CDs were obtained with some modifications based on previously reported methods [21,22]. Briefly, 0.5 g of citric acid anhydrous was dispersed in 10 mL of APTES and added into a 30 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave, and degassed with nitrogen for about 20 min. Then, the autoclave was firmly sealed and kept at 210 °C for 5 h for the carbonization and surface functionalization. After that, the color of the solution turned from colorless to yellow, the crude product was removed by centrifugation. The as-prepared saline-functionalized CDs were collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

CDs@MIPs was prepared via a sol–gel process at the surface of saline-functionalized CDs according to literature [23]. First, 100 mg of CA was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol in a 100 mL three-necked flask with the help of ultrasound, then 400 μL of APTES was added and the solution was stirred for 0.5 h in the nitrogen atmosphere. After that, 500 μL of CDs prepared above and 990 μL of TEOS were added under stirring. The polymerization was initiated by the addition of the mixture of 200 μL of ammonia solution and 800μL of water, persisted for 24 h. The resultant particles were centrifuged and washed with MeOH/HAc (90:10, v/v) by ultrasound and centrifugation alternately until no templates were detected from the washing solvent by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. CDs@MIPs were obtained after dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. CDs@NIPs were prepared following the same procedure of CDs@MIPs except the addition of the template molecules CA.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out with the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 700 V. The obtained dried powder of CDs@MIPs was dissolved into ultrapure water to make CDs@MIPs working solution (1.25 mg/mL). 500 μL of CDs@MIPs working solution prepared above was mixed with 500 μL of CA standard solution or samples containing CA, and vortexed. Then, the fluorescence of the mixture was measured. The control experiments of CDs@NIPs were as the same as that of CDs@MIPs.

For plasma sample analysis, blank human plasma samples were collected from healthy laboratory volunteers. 3 mL of acetonitrile was added to 500 μL of sample to remove the proteins in plasma [6]. After shaken vigorously for 5 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatant was concentrated to dry under nitrogen, then 500 μL different amounts of CA was added to redissolve the residual to prepare a series of spiked samples. Then, the samples were analyzed according to the procedure in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of saline-functionalized CDs and CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Silane-functionalized CDs were obtained via a simple one-pot hydrothermal process, as shown in Scheme 1 . The acylation reaction and surface passivation occurred simultaneously in the presence of APTES. In the process of silane-functionalized CDs synthesis, anhydrous citric acid was carbonized under high temperature conditions. Surface passivation was achieved via the acylation reaction of the amine group in APTES with the carboxyl groups on the surface of pyrolyzed organics. As a result, the surface silane-functionalized CDs with bright luminescence were obtained. CA template molecules combined with APTES through hydrogen bond interactions and Van der Waals forces.

After that, the polymerization took place on the surface of silane-functionalized CDs through the hydrolysis reaction of APTES and TEOS under the catalysis of aqueous ammonia. Consequently, CDs@MIPs were successfully obtained by embedding the template molecules in the silica-coated matrix [24]. After removal of the redundant reagents and template molecules by solvent elution, CDs@MIPs with specific CA recognition sites were obtained.

The eluent solvent used to wash CA from the raw CDs@MIPs was selected. We tried different eluents including methanol, methanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v), methanol/water (90:10, v/v), ethanol, ethanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) and ethanol/water (90:10, v/v) to wash the templates. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs recovered to the highest by using methanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) as an eluent. So, methanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) was selected as the eluent solvent in our further experiment.

The eluent solvent used to wash CA from the raw CDs@MIPs was selected. We tried different eluents including methanol, methanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v), methanol/water (90:10, v/v), ethanol, ethanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) and ethanol/water (90:10, v/v) to wash the templates. As shown in Fig. S1 , the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs recovered to the highest by using methanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) as an eluent. So, methanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) was selected as the eluent solvent in our further experiment.

In order to synthesize CDs@MIPs with high quality, the influence of the amount of APTES and TEOS and the reaction time on the sensitivity and selectivity of CDs@MIPs were optimized, and the optimal conditions to synthesize CDs@MIPs are displayed in detail in Supplementary material (S1).

In order to synthesize CDs@MIPs with high quality, the influence of the amount of APTES and TEOS and the reaction time on the sensitivity and selectivity of CDs@MIPs were optimized, and the optimal conditions to synthesize CDs@MIPs are displayed in detail in Supplementary material (S1).
The morphology of silane-functionalized CDs was characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 A showed that the obtained CDs presented uniform spherical morphology with an average diameter of 4.8 nm.

FT-IR spectra of APTES and silane-functionalized CDs are displayed in Fig. 1B. Compared with APTES, FT-IR spectrum of silane-functionalized CDs displayed two different absorption peaks. The peak at 1667 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of secondary amide carbonyl (CO), and 1576 cm−1 was attributed to secondary amine R-NH-R bending vibration. These two different signals revealed the formation of amide bonding (CONH), which demonstrated the successful acylation reaction between CDs and APTES, suggesting the successful synthesis of silane-functionalized CDs. The elementals of silane-functionalized CDs were analyzed by XPS and displayed in Fig. 1C. The full range analysis clearly showed four peaks at 101.62, 398.65, 283.94, and 531.43 eV, which were ascribed to Si2p, N1s, C1s, and O1s, respectively. The results of XPS further confirmed that CDs were successfully silane-functionalized.

The optical properties of silane-functionalized CDs were evaluated with UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1D). There was an absorption peak located at 354 nm in UV–vis absorption spectrum. Under excitation at the wavelength from 320 nm to 450 nm, silane-functionalized CDs showed strong blue photoluminescence in the emission spectra and their emission spectra were highly dependent on the excitation wavelength. The fluorescent intensity gradually increased with increasing excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 360 nm. The photoluminescence spectra showed the highest fluorescence intensity when excited at 360 nm. However, the fluorescent intensity gradually decreased as the excitation wavelength continued increasing.

The morphology of CDs@MIPs was characterized by TEM, and the results were shown in Fig. 2 A. The appearance of CDs@MIPs displayed spherical structure with a mean diameter of about 140 nm. The optical properties of CDs@MIPs were also studied. As shown in Fig. 2B, UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@MIPs (a) displayed a characteristic absorption peak around 250 nm–300 nm, which was corresponding to the aromatic π system belonging to CDs, and it was considered similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon [25]. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs@MIPs are shown in Fig. 2B (b) and Fig. 2B (c), respectively. It can be seen that a symmetric fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm was obtained when excited at 360 nm, which was similar to the silane-functionalized CDs characterized above. This indicates that the silica coating at the surface of CDs does not restrict the photoluminescence properties of CDs since the silica is optically transparent and inert [26].

The photoluminescence stability of CDs@MIPs is an important indicator concerning whether the material can be used in actual detection. Fig. 2C exhibited the stability of the CDs@MIPs. The result showed that the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs@MIPs exhibited a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.10% within six hours, which indicated the great stability of CDs@MIPs against photobleaching.

To ascertain the successful synthesis of CDs@MIPs, FT-IR spectra between CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs was compared. As shown in FT-IR spectrum of CDs@MIPs before templates extraction (Fig. 3 A (a)), the peak at 1488 cm−1 was ascribed to the aromatic ring stretching vibration of CA molecules, and this characteristic peak of CA disappeared in FT-IR spectrum of CDs@MIPs after templates extraction (Fig. 3A (b)), which revealed that CA molecules were successfully imprinted into CDs@MIPs and the template molecules could be effectively washed out through solvent elution method. FT-IR spectrum of CDs@NIPs (Fig. 3A(c)) was similar to that of CDs@MIPs after templates removed. The peaks around 1061 cm−1 and 792 cm−1 were ascribed to Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching and Si-O vibrations, respectively. These bands further confirmed that MIPs were successfully grafted on the surface of silane-functionalized CDs.

As shown in Fig. 3B, CDs@NIPs showed strong fluorescence while the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs before templates extraction was very weak. After the template molecules were washed out, the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs recovered nearly to that of CDs@NIPs. When CA molecules were further added, the fluorescence of CD@MIPs quenched a lot again. This phenomenon can be explained as charge transfer between CDs@MIPs and CA molecules [23,27]. In this recognition system, CDs@MIPs and CA molecules acted as electron acceptor and electron donor, respectively.

To evaluate the influence of the concentration of CDs@MIPs on the sensitivity of CDs@MIPs towards CA, the quenching efficiency of different concentrations of CDs@MIPs after adding 100 μM of CA was investigated, and the results are given in Fig. S2. The quenching efficiency was highest when CDs@MIPs was 1.25 mg/mL. So, 1.25 mg/mL CDs@MIPs were used in further experiments.

To evaluate the influence of the concentration of CDs@MIPs on the sensitivity of CDs@MIPs towards CA, the quenching efficiency of different concentrations of CDs@MIPs after adding 100 μM of CA was investigated, and the results are given in Fig. S2. The quenching efficiency was highest when CDs@MIPs was 1.25 mg/mL. So, 1.25 mg/mL CDs@MIPs were used in further experiments.

To compare the affinity to target CA molecules between CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs, the effects of a series of CA concentrations on the fluorescence of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs was studied. Fig. 4 A and B revealed the fluorescence intensity changes of both CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs with the increase of CA concentration, respectively. Although both CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs showed fluorescence quenching response to CA, the fluorescent intensity of CDs@MIPs was quenched much more remarkably than that of CDs@NIPs with increasing the concentration of CA.

The fluorescence quenching can be quantificationally described by Stern–Volmer equation as follows:
F 0
/ F = 1 + K
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher, and K is the Stern–Volmer constant. Fig. 4C displayed the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs or CDs@NIPs and CA concentrations in the range of 0.5 μM to 200 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) of CDs@MIPs was 0.11 μM while the LOD of CDs@NIPs was 0.34 μM, which was calculated according to the 3σ/K criterion (σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 10), and K is the slope of calibration graph). The extent of quenching and selectivity of CDs@MIPs can be estimated by K and imprinting factor (IF, the ratio of KMIPs to KNIPs), respectively. The K of CDs@MIPs and CDs@NIPs were 0.0034 and 0.0011, respectively. IF was calculated to be 3.09. These results manifested that the CDs@MIPs were much more sensitive for the template molecules than CDs@NIPs. This mainly attributed to the selective binding CA at specific sites in CDs@MIPs while there was only physical adsorption of CA in CDs@NIPs.

Furthermore, the reusability of the CDs@MIPs was investigated, and the results were shown in Fig. 4D. The removing-rebinding cycles were repeated using the same CDs@MIPs, the fluorescence was recovered by eluting (the up dots) and quenched by rebinding CA (the bottom dots). The results demonstrated that the CDs@MIPs could be repeatedly used at least five times without a significant decrease in the determination capacity for CA.

Linear range and LOD of this method were compared with the reported determination methods and the results are given in Table 1 . The method we established offered a better linear range and LOD than the most of the methods reported previously.

The fluorescence responses of CDs@MIPs to various interferents in plasma, including Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, glucose, galactose, Gly, Cys and BSA were investigated to evaluate the practical applicability of our proposed biosensor platform for CA detection. The concentration of BSA was 1 mg/mL, and the concentrations of the other interfering substances were all 1 mM, which were 10 folds higher than that of CA. As shown in Fig. 5 , the response of CDs@MIPs towards CA was much larger than that of the interfering substances, which indicated a selective adsorption of CDs@MIPs for the template molecules. The fluorescence response of CDs@NIPs towards CA was similar to those of the interfering substances in plasma, indicating that there were no selective recognition sites in CDs@NIPs. The results demonstrated that CDs@MIPs could be applied to the selective detection of CA molecules in plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CA in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability and validity of the developed CDs@MIPs biosensor, it was employed to detect CA in human plasma samples. Recovery studies were carried out by spiking the samples with CA in different concentration in the range of the linearity. The results are listed in Table 2 . The obtained recoveries were between 98.4% and 107.6% with RSDs in the range of 3.6–10.2%, which conformed the ability of the CDs@MIPs to provide accurate concentrations of CA in human plasma samples.

In this work, a simple and selective CDs@MIPs for recognition of CA was synthesized by a one-pot sol–gel process. The probe was easy to synthesize and simple to operate. This platform combined the virtue of the high sensitivity of CDs and the high selectivity of MIPs, and exhibited high specificity and affinity for CA. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MIPs decreased linearly with the increase of CA. This optical sensor demonstrated good accuracy in determination of trace CA in human plasma samples. In summary, this method was facile, low-costing, and had a potential application in clinical practice.
This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61775099, 21705080) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20171487, BK20171043).
The author(s) declare that they have no competing interests.
